title: Bold Jack Donahoo
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In Dublin town I was brought up, in that city of great fameן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My decent friends and parents, they will tell to you the same.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It was for the sake of five hundred pounds I was sent across the main,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For seven long years, in New South Wales, to wear a convictן¿½s chain.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Chorus

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Then come, my hearties, weן¿½ll roam the mountains high!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Together we will plunder, together we will die!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Weן¿½ll wander overֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ mountains and weן¿½ll gallop over plainsן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  For we scorn to live in slavery, bound down in iron chains.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½d scarce been there twelve months or more upon the Australian shore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When I took to the highway, as Iן¿½d oft-times done before.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There was me and Jacky Underwood, and Webber and Webster, too.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ These were the true associates of bold Jack Donahoo.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Chorus: Then come, &c.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now, Donahoo was taken, all for a notorious crime,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And sentenced to be hanged upon the gallows-tree so high.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But when they came to Sydney gaol, he left them in a stew,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when they came to call the roll, they missed bold Donahoo.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Chorus: Then come, &c.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As Donahoo made his escape, to the bush he went straight- way.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The people they were all afraid to travel night or dayן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For every week in the newspapers there was published some-thing new
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Concerning this dauntless hero, the bold Jack Donahoo!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Chorus: Then come, &c.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As Donahoo was cruising, one summerן¿½s afternoon,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ little was his notion his death was near so soon,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When a sergeant of the horse police discharged his car-a-bine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And called aloud on Donahoo to fight or to resign.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Chorus: Then come, &c.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Resign to youן¿½you cowardly dogs! a thing I neן¿½er will do,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For Iן¿½ll fight this night with all my might,ן¿½ cried bold Jack Donahoo.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½d rather roam these hills and dales, like wolf or kangaroo,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than work one hour for Government!ן¿½ cried bold Jack Donahoo.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Chorus: Then come, &c.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He fought six rounds with the horse police until the fatal ball,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which pierced his heart and made him start, caused Donahoo to fall.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And as he closed his mournful eyes, he bade this world Adieu,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Saying, ן¿½Convicts all, both large and small, say prayers for Donahoo!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Chorus: Then come, &c.
meaning:
"Bold Jack Donahoo" is a quintessential example of Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton)'s signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
